case_name: S. IYYAPAN Appellant (s) VERSUS M/S UNITED INDIA INSURANCE COMPANY LTD. AND ANOTHER Respondent(s)
citation: Civil Appeal No. 4834 of 2013 (Arising out of SLP (C) No.5091 of 2009); AIR 2013 SUPREME COURT 2262; (2013) 7 SCC 62
compensation_amount: Rs.2,42,400/- with interest at 12% p.a. from the date of petition

compensation_breakdown.tribunal_amount: Rs.2,42,400/- with interest at 12% p.a. from the date of petition
compensation_breakdown.final_amount: Rs.2,42,400/- with interest at 12% p.a. from the date of petition
compensation_breakdown.interest: 12% p.a. from the date of petition

facts: On 23.5.1998 at about 8:30 P.M. Charles, while riding a bicycle, was struck by a Mahindra Maxi Cab driven by Sivananayaitha Perumal who fled the scene. Charles was hospitalized and later succumbed to his injuries. Motor Vehicle Inspector’s Report showed no mechanical defect. The Motor Accidents Claims Tribunal at Kanyakumari awarded Rs.2,42,400/- with 12% interest from the date of petition to the deceased's dependants.
arguments_claimant: The claimant argued that a person holding a licence to drive a light motor vehicle is entitled to drive a light commercial vehicle such as a Mahindra Maxi Cab and relied on decisions including Ashok Gangadhar Maratha and Annappa Irappa Nesaria. The claimant contended the insurer remained liable to pay third-party compensation under the Motor Vehicles Act despite any licence endorsement issue.
arguments_respondent: The insurance company conceded the quantum but contested liability, arguing the driver did not hold an appropriate licence/endorsement to drive a commercial vehicle and relied on authorities such as New India Assurance v. Prabhu Lal and Sardari v. Sushil Kumar. The insurer urged that breach of the policy condition (driving by an unauthorized driver) absolved it of liability.
ratio_decidendi: The Court held that "the insurer cannot disown its liability on the ground that although the driver was holding a licence to drive a light motor vehicle but before driving light motor vehicle used as commercial vehicle, no endorsement to drive commercial vehicle was obtained in the driving licence." The Court emphasized the social welfare object of Chapter XI and that Section 149 makes it mandatory for the insurer "to satisfy the judgments and awards against persons insured in respect of third party risk," noting the insurer must pay "notwithstanding that the insurer may be entitled to avoid or cancel or may have avoided or cancelled the policy." The Court applied earlier decisions (Skandia; Sohan Lal Passi; National Insurance v. Swaran Singh) to hold that while Section 149(2)(a)(ii) permits an insurer to raise lack-of-licence as a defence, the insurer must prove breach by the insured and that such breach was fundamental and causative; mere absence of commercial endorsement on a licence to drive a light motor vehicle does not absolve the insurer. Finally, the Court held that where a valid driving licence to drive a light motor vehicle exists, "merely because the driver did not get any endorsement in the driving licence to drive Mahindra Maxi Cab... the High Court has committed grave error of law in holding that the insurer is not liable," and therefore the insurer must pay the award (and may seek recovery from the insured).
final_order: Appeal allowed; impugned High Court judgment set aside; insurer held liable to pay the compensation awarded to the dependants of the deceased (Rs.2,42,400/- with 12% p.a. interest from the date of petition). No order as to costs.
summary: This Supreme Court appeal concerned whether an insurer could avoid third-party liability because the driver had no separate endorsement to drive a light motor vehicle used as a commercial vehicle. The Court held that a valid licence to drive a light motor vehicle entitles the driver to drive a light commercial vehicle like a Mahindra Maxi Cab, and the insurer cannot escape liability solely for lack of endorsement. The original Tribunal award of Rs.2,42,400/- with 12% interest was upheld and the insurer was held liable to pay, with the insurer free to recover from the insured if appropriate.
embedding_summary: S. Iyyapan v. M/s United India Insurance Co. Ltd., Supreme Court of India, Civil Appeal No. 4834 of 2013 (SLP (C) No.5091/2009), judgment dated 1 July 2013. Accident on 23.05.1998 at Kanyakumari: Mahindra Maxi Cab (taxi) hit and killed Charles (bicyclist). Tribunal awarded Rs.2,42,400/- with interest at 12% p.a.; insurer contested liability arguing driver lacked endorsement to drive commercial vehicle. Key statutes: Section 149, 146, 147, 163A, 165, 168, 174 Motor Vehicles Act, 1988; key doctrines considered: statutory liability of insurer under Section 149(1), insurer’s defence under Section 149(2)(a)(ii), "pay and recover" approach, "rule of main purpose" and "fundamental breach" concept. Court relied on Skandia Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Kokilaben Chandravadan (1987) 2 SCC 654; Sohan Lal Passi (1996) 5 SCC 21; National Insurance Co. v. Swaran Singh (2004) 3 SCC 297; Annappa Irappa Nesaria (2008) 3 SCC 464; held insurer liable to pay tribunal award (Rs.2,42,400/- with 12% interest) and may recover from insured, clarifying that absence of commercial endorsement on a valid light motor vehicle licence does not absolve insurer.
legal_principles: Insurer's statutory liability under Section 149(1) to satisfy third-party judgments even if policy may be void or cancelled
Insurer may raise defence under Section 149(2)(a)(ii) (driving by person not duly licensed) but must prove breach by the insured and that breach was wilful/fundamental and causative
Absence of specific commercial endorsement on a licence to drive a light motor vehicle does not, by itself, absolve the insurer
Pay-and-recover doctrine: insurer pays award to third party and may recover sums from insured if policy conditions were breached
Interpretation of Chapter XI as social welfare legislation and 'rule of main purpose' to effectuate compensation to victims
cases_cited: Skandia Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Kokilaben Chandravadan, (1987) 2 SCC 654
Sohan Lal Passi v. P. Sesh Reddy & Ors., (1996) 5 SCC 21
Ashok Gangadhar Maratha v. Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd., AIR 1999 SC 3181 (1999 (6) SCC 620)
New India Assurance Company Ltd. v. Prabhu Lal, 2008 (1) SCC 696
Sardari & Ors. v. Sushil Kumar & Ors., 2008 ACJ 1307
New India Assurance Co. v. Kamla & Others, (2001) 4 SCC 342
National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Swaran Singh & Ors., (2004) 3 SCC 297
National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Kusum Rai & Others, (2006) 4 SCC 250
National Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Annappa Irappa Nesaria alias Nesaragi & Ors., 2008 (3) SCC 464
Oriental Insurance Co. Ltd. v. Nanjappan, (2004) (citation as in text)
bm25_text: S. IYYAPAN Appellant (s) VERSUS M/S UNITED INDIA INSURANCE COMPANY LTD. AND ANOTHER Respondent(s) Supreme Court of India 2013 On 23.5.1998 at about 8:30 P.M. Charles, while riding a bicycle, was struck by a Mahindra Maxi Cab driven by Sivananayaitha Perumal who fled the scene. Charles was hospitalized and later succumbed to his injuries. Motor Vehicle Inspector’s Report showed no mechanical defect. The Motor Accidents Claims Tribunal at Kanyakumari awarded Rs.2,42,400/- with 12% interest from the date of petition to the deceased's dependants. The claimant argued that a person holding a licence to drive a light motor vehicle is entitled to drive a light commercial vehicle such as a Mahindra Maxi Cab and relied on decisions including Ashok Gangadhar Maratha and Annappa Irappa Nesaria. The claimant contended the insurer remained liable to pay third-party compensation under the Motor Vehicles Act despite any licence endorsement issue. The insurance company conceded the quantum but contested liability, arguing the driver did not hold an appropriate licence/endorsement to drive a commercial vehicle and relied on authorities such as New India Assurance v. Prabhu Lal and Sardari v. Sushil Kumar. The insurer urged that breach of the policy condition (driving by an unauthorized driver) absolved it of liability. The Court held that "the insurer cannot disown its liability on the ground that although the driver was holding a licence to drive a light motor vehicle but before driving light motor vehicle used as commercial vehicle, no endorsement to drive commercial vehicle was obtained in the driving licence." The Court emphasized the social welfare object of Chapter XI and that Section 149 makes it mandatory for the insurer "to satisfy the judgments and awards against persons insured in respect of third party risk," noting the insurer must pay "notwithstanding that the insurer may be entitled to avoid or cancel or may have avoided or cancelled the policy." The Court applied earlier decisions (Skandia; Sohan Lal Passi; National Insurance v. Swaran Singh) to hold that while Section 149(2)(a)(ii) permits an insurer to raise lack-of-licence as a defence, the insurer must prove breach by the insured and that such breach was fundamental and causative; mere absence of commercial endorsement on a licence to drive a light motor vehicle does not absolve the insurer. Finally, the Court held that where a valid driving licence to drive a light motor vehicle exists, "merely because the driver did not get any endorsement in the driving licence to drive Mahindra Maxi Cab... the High Court has committed grave error of law in holding that the insurer is not liable," and therefore the insurer must pay the award (and may seek recovery from the insured). Appeal allowed; impugned High Court judgment set aside; insurer held liable to pay the compensation awarded to the dependants of the deceased (Rs.2,42,400/- with 12% p.a. interest from the date of petition). No order as to costs. This Supreme Court appeal concerned whether an insurer could avoid third-party liability because the driver had no separate endorsement to drive a light motor vehicle used as a commercial vehicle. The Court held that a valid licence to drive a light motor vehicle entitles the driver to drive a light commercial vehicle like a Mahindra Maxi Cab, and the insurer cannot escape liability solely for lack of endorsement. The original Tribunal award of Rs.2,42,400/- with 12% interest was upheld and the insurer was held liable to pay, with the insurer free to recover from the insured if appropriate. S. Iyyapan v.
